title: Regime-adaptive signal weights
hypothesis: Signal weights should vary by market regime — momentum dominates in bull markets (Jegadeesh & Titman), mean reversion and crash risk dominate in bear/volatile markets (DeBondt & Thaler, Ang et al. 2006). Fixed weights underweight the most predictive factor in each regime.
what_i_noticed: Signal engine uses fixed weights regardless of market regime (Bull/Bear/Volatile/Neutral)
89% feature drift is handled by reducing crash_prob weight, but weight reduction applies to default profile — not regime-aware
In bull market (current regime), momentum weight is only 0.12 despite momentum being the strongest factor in trends
In bear markets, mean_reversion weight is only 0.09 despite contrarian strategies being most profitable after large drops
8/12 stocks are Hold with low confidence — suggests signal differentiation could improve
what_i_did: Added regime_signal_weights to config.py with 3 regime-specific weight profiles (Bull, Bear, Volatile)
Bull profile: momentum 0.20 (up from 0.12), drawdown 0.15 (confirm trend), crash_prob 0.14 (less relevant in trends)
Bear profile: crash_prob 0.24 (critical), mean_reversion 0.16 (contrarian opportunities), momentum 0.06 (breaks down in bears)
Volatile profile: valuation 0.16 (VIX signals matter most), mean_reversion 0.14, momentum 0.08 (unreliable in whipsaws)
Neutral/Unknown fall through to default weights (backward compatible)
Modified signal_engine.py get_market_signal() to select weight profile based on regime parameter
Drift adjustment applies ON TOP of regime-selected weights (layered, not conflicting)
Added regime_weight_profile key to result dict for transparency
Wrote 15 tests covering: profile selection, behavioral effects, drift interaction, config validation, backward compatibility

before.signal_weights: Fixed: crash_prob=0.20, regime=0.16, valuation=0.11, momentum=0.12, mean_reversion=0.09, external=0.12, macro_risk=0.10, drawdown=0.10
before.regime_awareness: None — same weights in Bull, Bear, Volatile, Neutral

after.signal_weights: Regime-adaptive: Bull emphasizes momentum (0.20), Bear emphasizes crash_prob (0.24) + mean_reversion (0.16), Volatile emphasizes valuation (0.16) + mean_reversion (0.14)
after.regime_awareness: Full — weight profile selected by regime, drift layered on top

honest_analysis: This is a principled improvement grounded in factor investing research. The weight profiles are sensible but not empirically optimized — they're based on qualitative understanding of factor premia across regimes, not backtested weight optimization. The real test is whether these regime-adaptive weights improve signal accuracy on out-of-sample data. The implementation is minimal (3 lines of logic change) with full backward compatibility.
self_critique: Weight profiles are hand-tuned, not optimized via backtest — a future cycle could use walk-forward optimization
Only market-level weights are regime-adaptive; stock-level weights (analyst_target, options_iv, etc.) remain fixed
No empirical validation that these specific weight values improve Sharpe or hit rate
Could have also made the action thresholds regime-adaptive (e.g., higher threshold for Buy in bear markets)
next_steps: Walk-forward backtest to validate regime-adaptive weights vs fixed weights on historical data
Extend regime-adaptive concept to stock-level signal weights
Consider regime-adaptive action thresholds (harder to trigger Buy in bear markets)
Optimize weight profiles via grid search or Bayesian optimization within walk-forward framework
references: Jegadeesh & Titman (1993) — Momentum factor strongest in trending markets
DeBondt & Thaler (1985) — Mean reversion strongest after extreme moves
Ang et al. (2006) — Downside risk and volatility regime dependence
Asness, Moskowitz, Pedersen (2013) — Value and momentum everywhere (regime dependency)
